metaDescription: A 7-step framework for tendering a claim or lawsuit to your insurers: inventory every policy, tender promptly, check additional-insured status, and read the ROR letter.
summary: A practical seven-step framework, with a policy inventory, that helps in-house counsel and risk managers tender a claim or lawsuit to their liability insurers and get a carrier to fund the defense.
keywords: how to tender a claim to insurance carrier
duty to defend vs duty to indemnify
additional insured tender to vendor's carrier
independent counsel reservation of rights Cumis
consent to settle clause void coverage
which insurance policy responds to a lawsuit
D&O EPL professional liability claims-made reporting window
additional insured endorsement vs certificate of insurance

faq.question: What is the difference between the duty to defend and the duty to indemnify?
faq.answer: They are two different promises, and the defense one is far easier to trigger. In most states the duty to defend turns on whether the allegations are potentially within coverage — not on whether the claim has merit — so if any part of the complaint states a claim that could fall within the policy, the carrier generally must defend the entire suit, often subject to a reservation of rights. How far a court will look beyond the four corners of the complaint varies by state.

faq.question: Can my company be defended under another party's insurance policy?
faq.answer: Possibly. Vendor, supplier, contractor, and customer contracts routinely require the other side to name you as an additional insured and to defend or indemnify you for claims arising from their work, so if a claim traces to a counterparty's product, service, or premises, you may be entitled to a defense from their carrier — sometimes ahead of your own. Read the actual additional-insured endorsement, not just the certificate of insurance, because the endorsement's wording controls and is often narrower than the certificate suggests.

faq.question: What happens if I tender a claim to my insurer late?
faq.answer: Late notice is one of the most common ways to forfeit coverage you paid for. On claims-made policies the reporting window can be unforgiving; on occurrence policies, many states require the carrier to show prejudice before denying on late notice, but you do not want to be the case that tests where that line falls. The cost of an early tender is essentially zero, so when in doubt, notice it.

faq.question: What is a reservation-of-rights letter and when can I get independent counsel?
faq.answer: Most carriers agree to defend under a reservation of rights, preserving their ability to contest indemnity later, and the exclusions and defenses they reserve on signal where they intend to fight or deny. A genuine conflict between insurer and insured — for example, where the reservation gives the carrier an incentive to steer the defense toward uncovered theories — can entitle you to independent counsel at the carrier's expense rather than appointed panel counsel. Not every reservation creates that conflict, and the standard, the label (California calls it Cumis counsel), and who pays vary by state.

faq.question: Can I settle or admit liability on a claim without telling my insurer?
faq.answer: Most liability policies contain a consent-to-settle or no-voluntary-payments condition, and admitting liability, settling, or making payments without the carrier's consent can void coverage for that claim — even a settlement that looks like a bargain. Route settlement discussions through the carrier, get consent in writing, and document the carrier's position if it unreasonably withholds it.

faq.question: Should I tender a lawsuit I am confident I will win?
faq.answer: Yes — the duty to defend turns on what is alleged, not on what is true, so even a meritless suit can obligate the carrier to fund your defense if the allegations fall potentially within the policy. Tender first and litigate coverage later, because the defense dollars a carrier spends while it reserves rights are dollars you will not recover by being proven right on the merits a year later.
